content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Comrnission
July 30, 199 0
Honorable Richard Katz, chairman
AssenbLy committee on Transportation
State capitol
sacramento, cA 95814
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. r-9o-2 69
Dear Mr. Katz 3
rhis is in response to your request for advice regarding ap-
plication of the Political Reform Act (the 'Act")r to a proposed
contract for lobbyist compensation which is before the caLifornia-
Nevada cround super speed Transportation Commission (trsuper speed
Commissiont').
This l-etter is a follow-up to general- tel-ephone advice
provided to counsel for the Super Speed Commission, Thomas Knox,
on april 19, 1990. The following advice is based upon the revised
contract provided to us on April 20, 1990. As indicated to
Mr. John stevens of your staff in our telephone conversation of
ylay 2, 1990, we are treating your request as one for informal as-
siitance, because the guestions are general in nature.2 our
advice is Limited to provisions of the Act. we cannot provide
advice about other confl ict-of-interest 
1aws, such as Government
code 1o9o which prohibits a pub).ic official 
from having a
financial interest in a contract. 
You shoufd consult the Attorney
ceneraL about Section 1090.
1' 
Government code sections 81000-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indlcated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regrrlations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 rnformal assistance does not
irnrnunity provided by an opinion
83LL4; Regulation 18329 (c) (3). )
provide the requestor with the
or formal- written advice. (Section
428 J street, suite 8oo . p,o. Box goz . sacramento cA 959o4-ogo? o (916) 3 22.5660


our File No. f-9 0-2 69
Page 2
You have only requested advice concerning possible conflicts
of interest which nay arise as a result of tht proposed contract.3
You have not sought information concerning the requirernents of the
Act rrith respect to lobbyists and lobbyist employers. Therefore,
the foreqoing advice is linited to a discussion of conflict of
interest issues. Holrever, we are enclosing for your review a copy
of our informational rnanual on the lobbyinq disclosure provisions
of the Act.
OUESTION
Is the proposed lobbyist cornpensation contract perni.tted
under the Act?
CONCLUSION
The Act does not prohibit the Super speed commission frorn
entering into the proposed lobbyist conpensation contract.
However, the contract may create conflicts of interest in certain
situations.
FACTS
The Super Speed commission was created by legislation in 1988
and charged with the responsibility to develop a plan for super
speed train service betrreen southern California and Nevada ' 
we
h-ave previously detenained that the super speed comission is a
publi- entity for purposes of the Act. 
(Knox Advice Letter,
No. A-9O-038, coPY enclosed. )
Two individuals rrho wish to represent the Super Speed Cornrois-
sion professionally have fonded a separate nonprofit foundation
and will serve as the foundation's fundraisers. The foundation
will accept gifts in support of development of a super speed train
and, will, in turn, donate funds to the super speed conmission.
The Super Speed Conmission is considering entering into a lobbyist
compensation contract with these individuaLs.
ANALYSIS
The conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act prohibit a
public official 
fron naking, participating in naking or in any lray
attenpting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the public official 
knows or has
reason to know he or she has a financial interest. 
(Section
87100.) A publj-c official 
is defined, in part, as rrevery natural
person who is a member, officer, enployee or consultant of a state
or Iocal government agency.'r (Regulation 18700, copy enclosed.)
3 we cannot
( Regu lat ion
is timited
ass i-stance
render advice as to the duties of third parties.
18329 (c) (4) (c) , copy enclosed. ) Therefore, our advice
to a general overview which we hope wi).I be of sone
in evaluating the contract.


our File No. I-9o-2 69
Page 3
The Super Speed conmission is a governmental agency subject to the
Act. 
(Knox Advice Letter, supra.)
A person will be considered a consultant under the Act if he
or she provides information, advice, recommendation or counsel to
a governmental agency, unless suctr person: (1) is independent of
the control and direction of the agency, other than normal
contract roonitoring; and (2) possesses no authority with respect
to any agency decision beyond the rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel. (Regrulation 18700(a) (2);
Havden Advice Letter, No. A-84-319, copy enclosed.)
Individuals who are considered to be consultants are subject
to conflict of interest provisions of the Act:
t. 
They nust be included as designated enployees in the
conflict of interest code of the super Speed Coumission.' They
are required to disctose investments, interests, business posi-
tions ind sources of income and gifts which nay foreseeablye be
naterially affected by any of the decisions they nake as consult-
ants. (Sections a2o]-9, 87300 - 87313.)
2. They nay not nake, participate in rnaking or use their
official 
position to influence decisions which uay foreseeably and
naterially affect their economj.c interests. 
(Sections 87100,
87103. )
I{ithout more infornation, it is difficult 
to determine what
conflicts of interest nay arise from the proposed arrangement.
Therefore we can only provide a skeletal discussion of potential
problen areas.
Individuals who are contracting to provide their services to
the super Speed Conmission presumably have other clients. 
If it
j,s foreseeable that any of those clients who have been sources of
income to then of $250 or more within the past 12 months are
persons or entities who will be naterially affected by decisions
ot ttre super Speed Commission, the individuals nust disqualify
4 Th" code itself 
need only contain a general designation for
consultants. see Koppes Advice Letter, No. A-88-408, copy
enclosed.
5 Th" effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial tikelihood that they lri11 occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision must be nore than a nere possibil-
ity; 
hoerever, certainty is not required. 
(Doe/nev cares v. Downev
Communitv Devel,opment Con. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-99Li
witt v. llorrow (1977) 70 ca1 .App.3d 817, 822; fn re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed) . ) The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest,' it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. Morrow,
supra at 823. )


our FiIe No. I-9 0-2 69
Page 4
themselves from making, participating in or influencing such deci-
sj.ons. To the extent that the contract permits the super speed
conrnission to grant dispensation from disqual ification, 
it
confticts with the requireurents of the Act.
If the foundation to be formed by the individuals is a source
of income of 9250 or Dore (Section 82030) to them, they may not
Dake, participate in rnaking or influence decisions of the Super
Speed Connission if it is foreseeable that the decisions lri11 have
a material financial effect on the foundation.
Fina1ly, you have indicated that the funds raised by the
foundation are to be donated to the super Speed Commission. It is
possible that persons or entities that make donations to the
foundation nay be viewed as the true source of donations to the
Super Speed Commission. This could occur, for example, if the
conmissj.on were to deterurine that the foundation lras not really an
independent entity, but merely an intermediary for channeling
donations. In other circumstances, the Comnission has not
hesitated to pierce a corporate entity to reach a true source of
incoroe to further the purposes of the Act of fu11 disclosure.
(See, Hentschke Advice Letter No. A-80-03-59, copy enclosedl
Section 81002(c).) Additionally, the Act requires intermediaries
to disclose the actual donor, and requires designated employees to
report both the intermediary of a gift and the actual donor on
statenents of economic interest. 
(section 87313.)
In order to avoid conflicts of interest on the part of
individual nenbers of the suPer speed Comnission, care should be
taken to assure that any donatj,ons are nade to the Super Speed
Connission and not to its individual Dembers. we have previously
advised the super Speed Connission of the procedure set forth in
fn re Stone (L977) 3 FPPC Ops. 52 (copy enclosed) by which gifts
to a public entity will not be considered qifts to the public of-
ficials of that enti.ty.
If specific questions arise in the course of the contract,
please contact us and rre will be glad to assist you in revielring
then against the requirenents of the Act. If you have any ques-
ti.ons concerning the advice provided in this letter, please
contact ne at (916) 322-59OL.
sincerely,
scott HaLlabrin

Enclosures
Acting Generaf counsel

By: Margaret
counsel ,
W. Ellison
Legal Division

However, the contract may create conflicts of interest in certain
situations.
